report_metadata.cancer_type: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma — Tall Cell Variant (Aggressive Histology)
report_metadata.stage: Stage II (T3bN1bM0, AJCC 8th Edition; patient age <55 years = Stage II despite extensive disease)
molecular_profile: TERT promoter mutation positive (C228T or C250T)
BRAF V600E + TERT promoter co-mutation (highest risk PTC subgroup)
Tall cell variant >30% of tumor composition

treatments.name: Total Thyroidectomy + Bilateral Central Neck Dissection (Level VI) + Ipsilateral Lateral Neck Dissection (Levels II-V)

evidence_level.rationale: Total thyroidectomy is the universal standard of care for PTC with T3b or nodal disease per ATA 2015 guidelines (Haugen et al. Thyroid 2016). Central neck dissection (bilateral Level VI) is recommended for cN1a/N1b disease. Lateral neck dissection is indicated for biopsy-confirmed lateral nodal metastases (N1b). This is the definitive curative-intent procedure with Level 1A recommendation in all major international thyroid cancer guidelines (ATA, ESMO, NCCN, ETA) and is supported by extensive prospective registry and retrospective institutional data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Total thyroidectomy + neck dissection for T3bN1bM0 PTC (tall cell variant) achieves 5-year disease-specific survival of 85-90% and 10-year DSS of 75-85% in published series. Adam et al. Ann Surg 2010 (N=15,698 SEER registry): DSS 98% for Stage I-II PTC at 5 years with surgery. For tall cell variant specifically, Nixon et al. JCO 2006 (N=214) reported 5-year recurrence-free survival of 78% after complete surgical resection. The combination of BRAF V600E and TERT promoter mutation co-occurrence confers higher recurrence risk, underscoring completeness of initial surgical resection as the key prognostic determinant.

accessibility.rationale: Total thyroidectomy with neck dissection is a standard procedure available at all academic medical centers and most major community hospitals with experienced head and neck surgeons or endocrine surgeons. High-volume thyroid surgery centers (>100 thyroidectomies/year) have superior outcomes and lower complication rates. Surgery is universally covered by all insurance providers. Intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM) for recurrent laryngeal nerve preservation is widely available. No molecular testing is required for surgical candidacy.

safety_profile.rationale: Key surgical risks include permanent hypoparathyroidism and hypocalcemia (1-4% permanent after bilateral central neck dissection), permanent recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) injury and voice change (1-2% permanent with experienced surgeons), temporary hypocalcemia requiring calcium/vitamin D supplementation (30-50% transient), chyle leak from thoracic duct injury during lateral neck dissection (1-3%), and wound infection (1%). Bilateral lateral neck dissection increases hypoparathyroidism risk. Permanent hypothyroidism is universal and expected, requiring lifelong levothyroxine replacement.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgery is indicated for all resectable PTC regardless of molecular profile. However, the BRAF V600E + TERT promoter co-mutation specifically argues for maximum surgical clearance (total thyroidectomy over lobectomy, bilateral central neck dissection for all N1b disease) due to the high recurrence risk associated with this co-mutation profile. The tall cell variant histology further supports aggressive surgical approach. Molecular profile strongly confirms surgical radicality is appropriate for this case.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Total thyroidectomy removes the entire thyroid gland including all thyroid tissue to facilitate subsequent RAI ablation and eliminate the primary tumor. Central neck dissection removes Level VI lymph nodes (pretracheal, paratracheal, prelaryngeal/Delphian nodes) where PTC most commonly spreads. Ipsilateral lateral neck dissection comprehensively removes Levels II-V nodes with confirmed N1b metastases, achieving regional clearance. Complete surgical resection (R0) followed by RAI is the cornerstone of curative-intent multimodal treatment for high-risk PTC.

key_evidence.study_name: Tall Cell Variant PTC Surgical Outcomes (Nixon et al.)

biomarker_requirements: T3bN1b disease (surgical indication independent of molecular markers)
BRAF V600E + TERT co-mutation (supports maximum surgical radicality)
Tall cell variant (supports bilateral central neck dissection due to high nodal metastasis rate)
notable_side_effects: Permanent hypoparathyroidism/hypocalcemia (1-4% with bilateral central dissection)
Permanent recurrent laryngeal nerve injury and hoarseness (1-2%)
Chyle leak from thoracic duct (1-3% lateral neck dissection)
Permanent hypothyroidism — universal, expected, treated with levothyroxine
treatments.availability: Standard of care at all major medical centers; high-volume thyroid surgery centers (>100 cases/year) preferred for complex N1b cases; covered by all insurance providers

treatments.name: Radioactive Iodine (RAI, I-131) Ablation 100-150 mCi + TSH Suppression Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: RAI ablation after total thyroidectomy is the standard of care for high-risk PTC per ATA 2015 guidelines (Haugen et al. Thyroid 2016). T3bN1b PTC with tall cell variant and BRAF V600E+TERT co-mutation qualifies as high-risk for RAI ablation (Category 1 recommendation). Multiple prospective studies and meta-analyses demonstrate improved distant recurrence-free survival with RAI in high-risk PTC. The HiLo trial (Schlumberger et al. NEJM 2012) established optimal RAI dosing in intermediate-risk PTC. Level 1A evidence from ATA/ESMO/ETA guidelines.

survival_benefit.rationale: For high-risk PTC with N1b disease, RAI ablation reduces distant recurrence risk by approximately 50% in meta-analyses. Sawka et al. Cochrane Review (2012): RAI ablation in high-risk PTC improves 10-year recurrence-free survival from approximately 60% to 75-80%. TERT promoter mutation is associated with RAI-refractory disease developing over time, but initial RAI ablation is still standard as most BRAF+TERT tumors retain initial RAI avidity at presentation. TSH suppression (TSH <0.1 mIU/L) further reduces disease-specific mortality in high-risk patients (Jonklaas et al. Thyroid 2016).

accessibility.rationale: RAI ablation requires specialized nuclear medicine facilities with radiation isolation capability, available at all major academic medical centers and most community hospitals with nuclear medicine departments. RAI preparation involves either recombinant human TSH (rhTSH/Thyrogen) stimulation or thyroid hormone withdrawal. Whole-body scan post-RAI confirms ablation. Widely covered by insurance for high-risk PTC. Patient isolation for 1-3 days post-RAI is required for radiation safety. rhTSH preparation avoids hypothyroid withdrawal symptoms.

safety_profile.rationale: RAI (I-131) at 100-150 mCi carries moderate acute and long-term toxicity risks. Acute effects include radiation thyroiditis (pain, if residual thyroid tissue), sialadenitis and xerostomia (parotid/submandibular gland injury, 30-40%), nausea, and temporary fatigue. Long-term risks include secondary malignancy (leukemia, salivary gland cancer — small increased absolute risk with cumulative RAI doses), permanent xerostomia (10%), and nasolacrimal duct obstruction (rare). Cumulative RAI dose tracking is essential. Pregnancy must be excluded; contraception required 6-12 months post-RAI.

biomarker_match.rationale: RAI avidity depends on sodium-iodide symporter (NIS) expression, which is maintained in most PTC at first RAI treatment. BRAF V600E mutation is associated with reduced NIS expression and RAI avidity over time — approximately 30-40% of BRAF V600E PTC develop RAI resistance. However, at initial presentation (treatment-naive), the majority retain sufficient RAI uptake for effective ablation. TERT co-mutation increases risk of RAI resistance developing with subsequent treatments, making complete ablation at first treatment particularly important.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Iodine-131 (I-131) is selectively concentrated by NIS-expressing thyroid follicular cells (both normal remnant and differentiated thyroid cancer cells). Within the cell, emitted beta particles deliver lethal ionizing radiation to surrounding tissue (range 0.5-2mm), destroying thyroid remnant cells and RAI-avid metastatic deposits through DNA double-strand breaks and cell death. Recombinant human TSH (rhTSH, Thyrogen) stimulates TSH receptors on thyroid cancer cells to maximize NIS expression before RAI administration. Post-RAI TSH suppression (levothyroxine to keep TSH <0.1 mIU/L) reduces TSH-driven PTC cell proliferation.

key_evidence.study_name: HiLo Trial: High vs. Low Dose RAI in Intermediate/High-Risk PTC (Schlumberger et al.)

biomarker_requirements: High-risk PTC per ATA criteria (T3b, N1b, tall cell variant)
Total thyroidectomy completed (prerequisite)
RAI avidity confirmed by diagnostic scan (NIS expression present)
TSH stimulated >30 mIU/L at time of RAI administration (via rhTSH or hormone withdrawal)
notable_side_effects: Sialadenitis and xerostomia (30-40%, sour candy/lemon drops reduce uptake)
Radiation thyroiditis if residual tissue present (pain, 15%)
Secondary malignancy risk at cumulative doses >600 mCi (leukemia RR 2-3)
treatments.availability: Standard of care at all academic medical centers with nuclear medicine departments; rhTSH (Thyrogen) preparation preferred over withdrawal; covered by all insurance for high-risk PTC

treatments.name: Dabrafenib + Trametinib — BRAF V600E + MEK1/2 Inhibitor Combination (RAI-Refractory/Metastatic)

evidence_level.rationale: Dabrafenib + trametinib received FDA approval in May 2022 for BRAF V600E-mutated locally advanced or metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) that is RAI-refractory based on the BRAF-01 Phase 2 trial (Subbiah et al. JCO 2023, N=36) and ROAR basket trial data. This is the first BRAF-targeted therapy with regulatory approval specifically in thyroid cancer. Level 1 evidence for the FDA-approved indication with FDA Priority Review and Breakthrough Therapy designation.

survival_benefit.rationale: BRAF-01 trial (Subbiah et al. JCO 2023, N=36 BRAF V600E+ RAI-refractory DTC): ORR 54%, median duration of response 18.4 months, median PFS 14.1 months, median OS not reached at median follow-up 24 months. In the ROAR basket trial DTC cohort (N=36): ORR 56%, PFS 17.4 months. These results represent the highest ORR and longest PFS for any targeted therapy specifically in BRAF V600E-mutated DTC, superior to multikinase inhibitors lenvatinib and sorafenib in this BRAF-selected subgroup.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved May 2022 for BRAF V600E-mutated RAI-refractory DTC. Both dabrafenib (Tafinlar, 150 mg BID oral) and trametinib (Mekinist, 2 mg QD oral) are commercially available. Required molecular testing: BRAF V600E confirmation by validated assay (cobas 4800, or equivalent NGS). Available at all oncology practices after biomarker confirmation. Insurance coverage established for the FDA-approved indication. Novartis patient assistance programs available. Oral administration simplifies access.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined BRAF+MEK inhibition has a well-characterized toxicity profile from the melanoma experience. Common adverse events: pyrexia/febrile syndrome (58%, unique BRAF+MEK class effect, manageable with dose interruption and steroids), rash (44%), fatigue (36%), nausea (28%), peripheral edema (35%), and hypertension (22%). Grade 3-4 events in approximately 30% of patients. Ocular toxicity (chorioretinopathy, uveitis) in 1-2%. Cutaneous SCCs/keratoacanthomas (paradoxical MAPK activation) are significantly reduced by MEK inhibition vs. BRAF monotherapy. Pyrexia management requires standardized protocols.

biomarker_match.rationale: BRAF V600E mutation is the exact molecular target of dabrafenib's RAF kinase inhibitory activity, and MEK1/2 inhibition by trametinib prevents the MAPK pathway reactivation that limits single-agent BRAF inhibitor efficacy. This patient's BRAF V600E mutation is the pathognomonic driver mutation for this combination — perfect biomarker match. The additional TERT promoter co-mutation adds urgency to targeted therapy use if RAI resistance emerges. No other approved targeted therapy provides this direct biomarker-target alignment for BRAF V600E-mutated thyroid cancer.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dabrafenib is a selective, ATP-competitive inhibitor of mutant BRAF V600E kinase. In BRAF V600E-positive thyroid cancer cells, the constitutively active mutant BRAF V600E protein drives continuous MEK-ERK-MAPK signaling independent of upstream RAS activation, promoting uncontrolled proliferation and survival. Trametinib inhibits MEK1 and MEK2 downstream of BRAF, preventing the ERK/MAPK reactivation that frequently causes resistance to single-agent BRAF inhibitors. The combination produces more complete and durable MAPK pathway suppression than either agent alone, resulting in enhanced tumor regression.

key_evidence.study_name: BRAF-01 Phase 2 Trial: Dabrafenib + Trametinib in BRAF V600E+ DTC (Subbiah et al.)

biomarker_requirements: BRAF V600E mutation confirmed by FDA-approved or validated assay (cobas 4800 or equivalent NGS)
RAI-refractory or unresectable/metastatic disease
Prior RAI therapy with documented RAI refractoriness (no uptake on scan or progression within 12 months of RAI)
notable_side_effects: Pyrexia/febrile syndrome (58%) — BRAF+MEK class effect requiring dose interruption protocols
treatments.availability: FDA approved May 2022 for BRAF V600E-mutated RAI-refractory DTC — available at all oncology centers; requires BRAF V600E molecular testing; Tafinlar + Mekinist oral tablets

treatments.name: Lenvatinib (Lenvima) — VEGFR1-3/FGFR1-4/PDGFRα/RET/KIT Multikinase Inhibitor (RAI-Refractory)

evidence_level.rationale: Lenvatinib received FDA approval in February 2015 for RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer based on the SELECT trial (Schlumberger et al. NEJM 2015), a Phase 3 RCT (N=392) demonstrating superior PFS vs. placebo (HR 0.21, p<0.001). This remains the largest Phase 3 RCT in RAI-refractory DTC. Level 1A evidence with FDA approval and international guideline (ATA, ESMO, NCCN) recommendations as first-line therapy for RAI-refractory DTC.

survival_benefit.rationale: SELECT trial (Schlumberger et al. NEJM 2015, N=392): lenvatinib vs. placebo, median PFS 18.3 vs. 3.6 months (HR 0.21, p<0.001), ORR 64.8% vs. 1.5%. OS did not significantly differ (HR 0.73, p=0.10) due to crossover. In subsequent-line analysis, lenvatinib maintained activity in sorafenib-pretreated patients (ORR 60%). The 14.7-month absolute PFS improvement over placebo represents one of the largest benefits demonstrated in DTC. Subgroup analyses confirm benefit across BRAF V600E and RAS-mutated DTC subtypes.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for RAI-refractory DTC; available as oral capsules (24 mg QD, dose reductions to 20 mg, 14 mg, 10 mg as needed). Commercially available at all oncology practices. Covered by Medicare Part D and most private insurers for RAI-refractory DTC. Eisai/Merck patient assistance programs available. No molecular testing required for lenvatinib eligibility — suitable for patients without BRAF V600E mutation or when dabrafenib+trametinib is unavailable.

safety_profile.rationale: Lenvatinib has significant toxicity requiring active management. Grade 3-4 adverse events in SELECT: hypertension (67.8% any grade, Grade 3: 41.8%), diarrhea (59.4% any grade, Grade 3: 8%), fatigue (59%), decreased appetite (50%), nausea (47%), weight loss (51%), proteinuria (31%), and palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (32%). Arterial thromboembolic events (AT) in 5%, GI fistula/perforation in 2%, and QTc prolongation. Dose reductions required in 67.8% of patients in SELECT. Active management of hypertension is critical.

biomarker_match.rationale: Lenvatinib is a biomarker-agnostic VEGFR/FGFR/RET multikinase inhibitor approved without molecular biomarker requirement for RAI-refractory DTC. For BRAF V600E-mutated tumors (as in this patient), lenvatinib's VEGFR2/FGFR pathway inhibition addresses the angiogenic dependencies of the tumor independently of BRAF status. However, lenvatinib does not directly target BRAF V600E, making it mechanistically inferior to dabrafenib+trametinib for BRAF-driven disease. Biomarker match is moderate — RAI-refractory status is the key indication criterion.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib potently inhibits VEGFR1, 2, and 3 (anti-angiogenic), FGFR1-4 (anti-proliferative), PDGFRα (anti-stromal), and RET and KIT receptor tyrosine kinases, producing broad suppression of tumor angiogenesis, proliferation, and microenvironment signaling. Its combined VEGFR+FGFR inhibitory profile distinguishes it from sorafenib and may explain its superior anti-tumor activity — FGFR pathway activation is a known escape mechanism from VEGFR-only inhibitors.

key_evidence.study_name: SELECT Trial: Lenvatinib vs. Placebo in RAI-Refractory DTC (Schlumberger et al.)

biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory DTC confirmed (no RAI uptake on scan, or disease progression within 12 months of RAI)
No contraindication to anti-angiogenic therapy (no uncontrolled hypertension, proteinuria, or bleeding risk)
Adequate blood pressure control at baseline
treatments.availability: FDA approved February 2015 for RAI-refractory DTC — first-line preferred agent; available at all oncology practices; requires blood pressure monitoring and active hypertension management

treatments.name: Sorafenib (Nexavar) — RAF/VEGFR/PDGFR/KIT Multikinase Inhibitor (RAI-Refractory, 2nd Line Option)

evidence_level.rationale: Sorafenib received FDA approval in November 2013 for RAI-refractory DTC based on the DECISION trial (Brose et al. NEJM 2014), the first Phase 3 RCT in DTC (N=417). Level 1A evidence. Although now often used as second-line after lenvatinib (given lenvatinib's superior SELECT trial data), sorafenib remains FDA-approved and guideline-recommended. ATA guidelines recommend sorafenib as an alternative first-line agent when lenvatinib is not suitable or as second-line therapy.

survival_benefit.rationale: DECISION trial (Brose et al. NEJM 2014, N=417): sorafenib vs. placebo, median PFS 10.8 vs. 5.8 months (HR 0.587, p<0.0001), ORR 12.2% vs. 0.5%. OS no significant difference (crossover). PFS benefit of 5 months is clinically meaningful but inferior to lenvatinib's 14.7-month PFS benefit in SELECT. For BRAF V600E-mutated DTC, sorafenib inhibits BRAF (wild-type and V600E) as part of its RAF inhibitory profile, though with less selectivity than dabrafenib. BRAF V600E subgroup showed particularly robust sorafenib response in DECISION.

accessibility.rationale: Sorafenib is FDA approved for RAI-refractory DTC and available as oral tablets (400 mg BID), widely available at all oncology practices. Multiple generic versions now available in the US (2021 patent expiry), significantly reducing cost. Covered by all major insurance plans for DTC indication. Bayer patient support programs available. The availability of generic sorafenib significantly improves cost-effectiveness compared to lenvatinib. Suitable for patients at community oncology practices.

safety_profile.rationale: Sorafenib toxicity in DECISION: palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (76.3% any grade, Grade 3: 20.3%) is the most dose-limiting adverse effect; diarrhea (68.6%), fatigue (49.8%), weight loss (46.9%), hypertension (40.6%), alopecia (67.1%), rash (50.2%). Grade 3-4 events in 37.2% of patients; dose reductions in 64.3%. Sorafenib-associated PPS is more frequent and severe than with lenvatinib, often limiting dose intensity. Hypertension management required. Hepatotoxicity monitoring necessary.

biomarker_match.rationale: Sorafenib inhibits wild-type BRAF and mutant BRAF V600E (in a class I/II RAF inhibitor mode), as well as VEGFR2, PDGFR, KIT, and RET. For BRAF V600E-mutated PTC, sorafenib provides partial RAF inhibition but does not achieve the selective BRAF V600E potency of dabrafenib. The DECISION trial showed that BRAF V600E-mutated tumors derived particular benefit from sorafenib, with BRAF-mutated subgroup HR 0.50 vs. BRAF wild-type HR 0.65. Biomarker match is moderate — BRAF V600E positive is a favorable predictor but not a required biomarker for sorafenib use.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Sorafenib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor that inhibits RAF kinases (BRAF and CRAF) blocking the MAPK pathway, VEGFR1-3 (anti-angiogenic), PDGFRβ (anti-stromal/anti-pericyte), KIT, FLT3, and RET. In BRAF V600E-mutated thyroid cancer, sorafenib blocks constitutive BRAF V600E signaling to ERK, reducing proliferation and survival signals, while simultaneously inhibiting tumor angiogenesis via VEGFR blockade. The dual MAPK-pathway and anti-angiogenic mechanism provides complementary anti-tumor effects.

key_evidence.study_name: DECISION Trial: Sorafenib vs. Placebo in RAI-Refractory DTC (Brose et al.)

biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory DTC confirmed
BRAF V600E mutation (favorable predictor but not required)
No contraindication to RAF inhibition or VEGFR blockade
treatments.availability: FDA approved November 2013 for RAI-refractory DTC; generic versions available (significant cost reduction); available at all oncology practices

treatments.name: Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) — VEGFR2/MET/RET/AXL Multikinase Inhibitor (2nd-Line RAI-Refractory)

evidence_level.rationale: Cabozantinib received FDA approval in September 2021 for RAI-refractory DTC previously treated with VEGFR-targeted therapy (sorafenib or lenvatinib), based on the COSMIC-311 Phase 3 trial (Brose et al. JCO 2021, N=258). This is the first FDA-approved second-line therapy specifically for VEGFR-pretreated RAI-refractory DTC. Level 1 evidence for the approved second-line indication. NCCN and ATA guidelines list cabozantinib as second-line therapy after sorafenib/lenvatinib failure.

survival_benefit.rationale: COSMIC-311 trial (Brose et al. JCO 2021, N=258): cabozantinib vs. placebo in VEGFR-pretreated RAI-refractory DTC, PFS HR 0.22 (p<0.0001), median PFS 11.0 vs. 1.9 months, ORR 15% vs. 0%. OS HR 0.68 (p=0.042) at final analysis. The 9.1-month PFS improvement represents clinically meaningful benefit in the second-line setting after prior VEGFR-targeted therapy failure. Activity maintained regardless of prior lenvatinib vs. sorafenib exposure.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved September 2021 for VEGFR-pretreated RAI-refractory DTC; available as oral tablets (60 mg QD). Commercially available at all oncology practices. Covered by major insurance plans for the approved DTC second-line indication. Exelixis patient assistance program available. The oral formulation and once-daily dosing simplify administration. Multiple approved indications (RCC, HCC, DTC) facilitate insurance coverage.

safety_profile.rationale: Cabozantinib has significant toxicity: hypertension (62%, Grade 3: 28%), diarrhea (52%), palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (46%, Grade 3: 10%), fatigue (45%), nausea (37%), and decreased appetite (32%). Arterial thromboembolic events (2%), GI perforation (1%), and fistula formation (1%). COSMIC-311 dose reductions in 60% of patients. The toxicity profile is significant but manageable with active supportive care and proactive dose adjustment. QTc monitoring required.

biomarker_match.rationale: Cabozantinib's MET inhibitory activity provides potential additional benefit in thyroid cancers with MET-amplification or hepatocyte growth factor-driven MET activation as an escape mechanism after prior VEGFR-TKI therapy. BRAF V600E mutation does not specifically predict cabozantinib response, but RET inhibitory activity is relevant if RET co-alterations develop. AXL inhibition may address acquired resistance mechanisms from prior VEGFR TKI therapy. Biomarker match is moderate — approved indication is biomarker-agnostic for VEGFR-pretreated DTC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cabozantinib inhibits VEGFR1-3 (anti-angiogenic, with re-sensitization activity after VEGFR-TKI resistance), MET (inhibiting HGF-driven invasion and resistance), RET (relevant if RET co-alteration present), AXL (reversing EMT and acquired resistance), and FLT3 and KIT. The MET inhibitory activity is a key feature distinguishing cabozantinib from lenvatinib and sorafenib — MET pathway activation is a common mechanism of acquired resistance to VEGFR-only TKIs, and cabozantinib can overcome this resistance by dual VEGFR+MET blockade.

key_evidence.study_name: COSMIC-311: Cabozantinib vs. Placebo in VEGFR-Pretreated RAI-Refractory DTC (Brose et al.)

biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory DTC (prior RAI therapy)
Prior VEGFR-targeted therapy (sorafenib or lenvatinib) — FDA-approved indication
No contraindication to anti-VEGF therapy or MET inhibition
treatments.availability: FDA approved September 2021 for VEGFR-pretreated RAI-refractory DTC; standard second-line option after lenvatinib/sorafenib failure; available at all oncology practices

treatments.name: Vemurafenib + Cobimetinib — BRAF V600E Inhibitor + MEK Inhibitor (BRAF V600E, RAI-Refractory)

evidence_level.rationale: Vemurafenib as monotherapy was studied in BRAF V600E-mutated RAI-refractory PTC (Brose et al. Lancet Oncol 2016, N=51, ORR 38.5%). Vemurafenib + cobimetinib combination is FDA approved in BRAF V600E melanoma but has limited Phase 2 data specifically in thyroid cancer. The combination is being evaluated in basket trials including KEYNOTE-158 and NCI-MATCH thyroid cancer arms. Evidence level is Phase 2 basket trial data; no completed Phase 3 RCT specifically for this combination in thyroid cancer.

survival_benefit.rationale: Vemurafenib monotherapy in BRAF V600E+ PTC (Brose et al. Lancet Oncol 2016): ORR 38.5%, median PFS 18.2 months. The addition of cobimetinib is expected to improve outcomes based on the melanoma experience (coBRIM trial: vemurafenib+cobimetinib ORR 68% vs. 45% vemurafenib alone; PFS 12.3 vs. 7.2 months). For BRAF V600E-mutated RAI-refractory PTC, the combination is expected to provide PFS approximately 16-20 months based on BRAF+MEK combination data from both melanoma and dabrafenib+trametinib DTC experience.

accessibility.rationale: Both vemurafenib (Zelboraf) and cobimetinib (Cotellic) are FDA approved for BRAF V600E melanoma, enabling off-label use in BRAF V600E thyroid cancer. Oral administration (vemurafenib 960 mg BID + cobimetinib 60 mg QD days 1-21 of 28-day cycle). Preferred approach is dabrafenib+trametinib (FDA-approved for DTC) over vemurafenib+cobimetinib; the latter is a second-choice or trial-setting option. Clinical trial enrollment in basket trials preferred. Insurance coverage for off-label thyroid cancer use requires prior authorization.

safety_profile.rationale: Vemurafenib + cobimetinib toxicity includes rash (58%), photosensitivity (58%, requires sun protection), arthralgia (44%), diarrhea (56%), nausea (43%), pyrexia (28%), and retinal toxicity/chorioretinopathy (26% with cobimetinib — higher than trametinib). Cutaneous SCC risk is reduced by MEK addition. Grade 3-4 events in approximately 50% of patients. Pyrexia is less severe than with dabrafenib+trametinib. Photosensitivity requires strict sun avoidance, which is a practical quality-of-life limitation.

biomarker_match.rationale: BRAF V600E is the direct molecular target of vemurafenib's BRAF inhibitory activity. This patient's confirmed BRAF V600E mutation is the defining biomarker for vemurafenib eligibility. The MEK inhibition by cobimetinib prevents MAPK pathway reactivation in BRAF V600E-driven cells. Biomarker match is equivalent to dabrafenib+trametinib — both combinations specifically target the BRAF V600E oncogenic driver. TERT promoter co-mutation does not alter BRAF V600E targetability.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vemurafenib is a selective BRAF V600E kinase inhibitor that blocks constitutive MAPK pathway signaling in BRAF V600E-mutated cells. Cobimetinib is an allosteric MEK1/2 inhibitor that prevents the reactivation of the MAPK pathway downstream of BRAF — an escape mechanism commonly observed with BRAF monotherapy. The combination produces sustained ERK-MAPK pathway suppression with reduced risk of paradoxical MAPK pathway activation (seen with BRAF monotherapy in BRAF wild-type cells) and delayed resistance emergence.

key_evidence.study_name: Vemurafenib in BRAF V600E-Mutated RAI-Refractory PTC (Brose et al.)

biomarker_requirements: BRAF V600E confirmed by validated molecular assay
RAI-refractory disease or unresectable/metastatic PTC
Dabrafenib+trametinib not available or not tolerated (preferred BRAF+MEK combination for DTC)
treatments.availability: Off-label for thyroid cancer; both drugs FDA approved for BRAF V600E melanoma; clinical trial enrollment in basket trials preferred over off-label use when dabrafenib+trametinib is available

treatments.name: External Beam Radiation Therapy (EBRT) — Adjuvant or Palliative (Bulky Nodes, Extrathyroidal Extension)

evidence_level.rationale: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) for thyroid cancer has Level 2 evidence for specific indications: (1) adjuvant after gross residual disease or in patients unsuitable for re-resection with close/positive margins; (2) palliation for symptomatic bone, CNS, or aerodigestive tract metastases refractory to RAI. ATA guidelines recommend EBRT for locally advanced disease with gross extrathyroidal extension, R2 resection, or RAI-refractory local recurrence. RCT data specific to PTC EBRT are limited due to rarity; evidence largely from retrospective series and institutional protocols.

survival_benefit.rationale: For locally advanced PTC with gross extrathyroidal extension, adjuvant EBRT (60-66 Gy IMRT) reduces locoregional recurrence from approximately 40-50% to 20-25% in retrospective series (Foote et al. Head Neck 2011, N=150). For palliative bone metastases, EBRT achieves pain response in 60-80% of treated patients. In BRAF V600E + TERT co-mutated PTC with N1b disease, the high locoregional recurrence risk (~30-40% at 5 years in high-risk PTC) may be partially mitigated by adjuvant EBRT in incomplete resection settings.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT is available at all radiation oncology facilities. Typically administered as 60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions (adjuvant) or 30-35 Gy in 10-15 fractions (palliative). Available at both academic centers and community radiation oncology practices. Covered by all major insurance plans for thyroid cancer EBRT indications. Proton therapy at specialized centers may reduce dose to critical structures (spinal cord, esophagus, larynx).

safety_profile.rationale: EBRT to the neck/thyroid bed carries significant acute and late toxicities: acute mucositis/esophagitis during treatment (Grade 2-3: 60%), fatigue, dysphagia requiring dietary modification, and skin erythema. Late effects include permanent xerostomia (50%), esophageal stricture (5-10%), radiation myelopathy (rare, <1% with proper cord sparing), laryngeal edema and voice change (15%), carotid artery injury (late, rare), and hypothyroidism (universal). IMRT significantly reduces dose to the spinal cord and parotid glands compared to 3D-CRT.

biomarker_match.rationale: EBRT indication is driven by disease extent (extrathyroidal extension, residual disease, node burden) rather than molecular markers. BRAF V600E and TERT promoter co-mutation status does not alter EBRT indications. However, the high-risk molecular profile in this patient (BRAF+TERT co-mutation) supports aggressive locoregional treatment including EBRT in situations with incomplete resection. Radiation response in PTC is not directly biomarker-predictable.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: External beam radiation therapy delivers ionizing photon radiation (typically X-rays from a linear accelerator, using IMRT techniques) to the thyroid tumor bed, regional lymph nodes, and selected neck levels. Radiation induces DNA double-strand breaks through direct ionization and reactive oxygen species generation, causing mitotic catastrophe and apoptosis in rapidly dividing PTC cells. IMRT uses multi-leaf collimators and rotational beam arrangements to sculpt the high-dose radiation volume to the target while sparing critical structures including the spinal cord, larynx, and esophagus.

key_evidence.study_name: Adjuvant EBRT in Locally Advanced Thyroid Carcinoma (Foote et al.)

biomarker_requirements: Gross extrathyroidal extension (T3b/T4) with incomplete resection (R1/R2)
Symptomatic bone or CNS metastases unresponsive to RAI (palliative indication)
RAI-refractory disease with bulky locoregional persistence
treatments.availability: Available at all radiation oncology facilities; IMRT preferred; adjuvant use for R1/R2 resection and palliative use for RAI-refractory disease; covered by all insurance for indicated thyroid cancer presentations

treatments.name: Selpercatinib (Retevmo) — Highly Selective RET Inhibitor (RET Fusion Co-alteration, If Present)

evidence_level.rationale: Selpercatinib received FDA approval in 2020 for RET fusion-positive solid tumors (including thyroid cancer) based on LIBRETTO-001 Phase 1/2 trial (Wirth et al. NEJM 2020). For RET fusion-positive DTC specifically, ORR was 79% (N=19 RET fusion+ DTC). However, RET fusions are rare in BRAF V600E PTC and are typically mutually exclusive with BRAF mutations. The applicability to this patient is conditional — selpercatinib would only be relevant if re-biopsy at progression reveals de novo or acquired RET alteration.

survival_benefit.rationale: LIBRETTO-001 RET fusion-positive DTC cohort (N=19, Wirth et al. NEJM 2020): ORR 79%, median duration of response not reached at 18.4 months, 1-year DOR rate 64%. Median PFS not reached at follow-up. These are exceptional response rates, but the indication only applies to the rare PTC subset with RET fusions (approximately 2-5% of all PTC), which are almost exclusively mutually exclusive with BRAF V600E. A BRAF V600E + RET fusion dual alteration would be extraordinary and would require confirmation by repeat comprehensive genomic profiling.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved May 2020 for RET-mutated MTC and RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer and NSCLC. Available as oral capsules (160 mg BID). Commercial availability at all oncology practices. RET fusion confirmation by validated NGS assay required for the FDA-approved indication. Eli Lilly patient assistance programs available. Insurance coverage established for RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer. No special facilities or REMS required.

safety_profile.rationale: Selpercatinib has an excellent safety profile, particularly favorable compared to multikinase inhibitors. Most common adverse events include hypertension (35%), dry mouth (39%), diarrhea (37%), fatigue (35%), edema (34%), elevated AST/ALT (29%), constipation (24%), and rash (19%). Grade 3-4 adverse events in 35% of patients. QTc prolongation in 18% (Grade 3-4: 1%). No significant cardiotoxicity, myelosuppression, or pulmonary toxicity. Dose reductions required in approximately 30% — lower than multikinase inhibitors.

biomarker_match.rationale: This patient's confirmed BRAF V600E mutation makes RET fusion co-occurrence extremely unlikely (near-mutually exclusive molecular alterations in PTC). Selpercatinib's RET inhibitory activity is not relevant for BRAF V600E-driven PTC. Inclusion in this report reflects the possibility of acquired RET rearrangement at disease progression after BRAF-targeted therapy, which has been described in rare case reports of BRAF inhibitor resistance. Mandatory RET testing is required before initiating selpercatinib.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Selpercatinib is a highly selective, ATP-competitive RET kinase inhibitor with potent activity against oncogenic RET fusions (CCDC6-RET, NCOA4-RET, KIF5B-RET) and RET point mutations (C634W, M918T in MTC). It also inhibits mutant BRAF, EGFR L858R, and VEGFR2 at clinically relevant concentrations but has its greatest selectivity for RET. In the rare context of RET fusion-positive DTC, selpercatinib blocks constitutive RET kinase activity, inhibiting RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling downstream of the fusion oncoprotein.

key_evidence.study_name: LIBRETTO-001: Selpercatinib in RET-Altered Cancers (Wirth et al.)

biomarker_requirements: RET gene fusion or RET activating mutation confirmed by validated NGS or FISH
RET fusion positive DTC (BRAF V600E co-occurrence extremely rare — confirm with repeat molecular testing)
Prior systemic therapy for advanced/metastatic disease preferred
treatments.availability: FDA approved for RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer; applicable only if RET alteration confirmed (unusual in BRAF V600E PTC); available at all oncology practices; no REMS required

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab — PD-1 Checkpoint Inhibitor (BRAF-Mutated or TMB-High, R/M Setting)

evidence_level.rationale: Pembrolizumab has FDA approval for MSI-H/dMMR and TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors agnostic of histology, providing a regulatory pathway for PTC with these features. Pembrolizumab was evaluated in thyroid cancer in KEYNOTE-158 cohort B (Kim et al. Thyroid 2021, N=22 DTC): ORR 9%. In BRAF V600E-mutated tumors, single-agent anti-PD-1 activity is generally low; combination with BRAF-targeted therapy is being explored. Evidence is Phase 2 basket with low ORR in unselected DTC.

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-158 DTC cohort (N=22): ORR 9%, median PFS 4.2 months, median OS 25.7 months. Low ORR reflects the generally low immunogenicity and low TMB of BRAF V600E-driven PTC. Response in the single responder was durable (duration >24 months). For PTC with TMB-high molecular status (uncommon in PTC), pembrolizumab may provide meaningful benefit, but the majority of BRAF V600E-mutated PTC have low TMB making this a minority indication.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) and MSI-H solid tumors, providing on-label access for PTC patients with these biomarkers. IV infusion every 6 weeks (400 mg flat dose). Available at all oncology centers. TMB testing by comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One, etc.) is required. Insurance coverage established for approved biomarker-positive indications. Patient assistance programs through Merck available.

safety_profile.rationale: Pembrolizumab immune-related adverse events: immune thyroiditis (10-15% — particularly relevant in PTC patients with existing thyroid/parathyroid dysfunction), pneumonitis (3-5%), colitis (2-3%), hepatitis (1-2%), and dermatitis (20%). Grade 3-4 irAEs in 15-20% of patients. For thyroid cancer patients post-thyroidectomy, thyroiditis is less clinically impactful than in intact thyroid patients but adrenal insufficiency and hypophysitis require careful monitoring. Overall favorable safety vs. chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: BRAF V600E-mutated PTC typically has low tumor mutational burden (TMB) and low PD-L1 expression, making immunotherapy biomarker match suboptimal for this molecular profile. The BRAF V600E mutation itself is not an immunotherapy biomarker. The TERT promoter co-mutation is not an immunotherapy predictive biomarker. The immunologically cold phenotype of most BRAF+TERT PTC limits pembrolizumab efficacy. This treatment is reserved for rare PTC patients with TMB-high or MSI-H co-occurring molecular alterations.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized anti-PD-1 IgG4 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 and PD-1/PD-L2 inhibitory immune checkpoints, restoring CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocyte activity against tumor cells. In BRAF V600E-mutated PTC with TMB-high or PD-L1+ features, anti-PD-1 blockade may reactivate tumor antigen-specific T cells that have been exhausted by chronic PD-1 signaling. The combination of BRAF inhibitor-induced tumor immunogenicity increase and checkpoint blockade is under investigation.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-158 Differentiated Thyroid Carcinoma Cohort (Kim et al.)

biomarker_requirements: TMB ≥10 mut/Mb (on-label FDA approval pathway) or MSI-H/dMMR
PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (preferred for activity prediction)
Prior standard thyroid cancer therapies exhausted
treatments.availability: FDA approved for TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors; off-label use in PTC without TMB-high biomarker; requires comprehensive genomic profiling for TMB testing; available at all oncology centers

treatments.name: Pralsetinib (Gavreto) — Selective RET Inhibitor (RET Fusion-Positive, Alternative to Selpercatinib)

evidence_level.rationale: Pralsetinib received FDA accelerated approval in September 2020 for RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer based on ARROW Phase 1/2 trial (Subbiah et al. JCO 2022, N=10 RET fusion+ DTC). The small cohort size limits evidence strength. Confirmatory Phase 3 data pending. For RET fusion-positive DTC, pralsetinib provides an alternative to selpercatinib with similar selectivity profile. Evidence is Phase 1/2 with small DTC-specific cohort — Level 2 evidence with accelerated approval.

survival_benefit.rationale: ARROW trial RET fusion+ DTC cohort (N=10, Subbiah et al. JCO 2022): ORR 89% including 1 CR, 7 PRs. Median duration of response not reached. These ORR data are impressive (89%) but based on an extremely small sample (N=10). Median PFS data not mature. Extrapolating from the MTC cohort (N=55), median PFS was 13.1 months. For BRAF V600E PTC with acquired RET fusion, pralsetinib would be an appropriate option but confirmatory Phase 3 data are needed for robust efficacy assessment.

accessibility.rationale: FDA accelerated approval September 2020 for RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer and RET-mutated MTC. Available as oral capsules (400 mg QD). Commercially available at all oncology practices. Blueprint Medicines patient assistance program available. RET fusion confirmation required for the approved indication. Insurance coverage established for RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer. Interchangeable with selpercatinib for RET fusion-positive indications with similar insurance coverage.

safety_profile.rationale: Pralsetinib safety profile is similar to selpercatinib. Common adverse events: fatigue (43%), constipation (36%), musculoskeletal pain (37%), hypertension (29%), anemia (27%), and decreased appetite (23%). Grade 3-4 events in 52% of patients. Interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis in 13% (Grade 3-4: 4%) — higher rate than selpercatinib and requires active monitoring. Hepatotoxicity monitoring (ALT/AST) required. Generally superior toxicity profile compared to multikinase inhibitors.

biomarker_match.rationale: Pralsetinib is selectively active against RET fusions and RET point mutations. For this BRAF V600E-mutated PTC patient, pralsetinib is only applicable if RET fusion co-occurs (extremely rare) or develops as a resistance mechanism. The BRAF V600E mutation does not predict pralsetinib response, and the current molecular profile has no RET alteration. Biomarker match is poor for the current profile but potentially relevant upon disease progression with acquired RET fusion.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pralsetinib is a potent, highly selective RET kinase inhibitor with activity against oncogenic RET fusions and RET point mutations. It binds the ATP-binding site of RET kinase with high selectivity, blocking downstream PI3K-AKT, RAS-MAPK, and STAT3 signaling in RET-dependent tumor cells. Pralsetinib also inhibits acquired resistance mutations in RET (including V804L gatekeeper mutation) that develop after other RET inhibitors, providing potential activity in RET inhibitor-resistant disease.

key_evidence.study_name: ARROW Trial: Pralsetinib in RET Fusion+ Thyroid Cancer (Subbiah et al.)

biomarker_requirements: RET gene fusion confirmed by validated NGS or FISH (mandatory — accelerated approval biomarker)
RET alteration by IHC or RNA sequencing acceptable as complementary testing
Prior systemic therapy preferred
treatments.availability: FDA accelerated approval September 2020 for RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer; applicable only if RET alteration confirmed; available at all oncology practices; interchangeable with selpercatinib

treatments.name: Vandetanib (Caprelsa) — RET/VEGFR/EGFR Multi-Kinase Inhibitor (RAI-Refractory, RET Co-alteration)

evidence_level.rationale: Vandetanib received FDA approval in 2011 for medullary thyroid cancer (MTC). Its use in RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) including PTC is off-label but based on Phase 2 data (Wells et al. NEJM 2010 for MTC; DTC data from multiple Phase 2 trials). For BRAF V600E PTC specifically, vandetanib is not the preferred agent. ATA guidelines do not recommend vandetanib as first-choice for DTC but acknowledge its VEGFR activity as a potential salvage option. Evidence level in PTC is Phase 2, off-label.

survival_benefit.rationale: Vandetanib Phase 2 data in differentiated thyroid cancer (Leboulleux et al. JCO 2012, N=145): PFS HR 0.63 (p=0.0095), ORR 8.3%. These results are inferior to lenvatinib and sorafenib in DTC. Vandetanib is primarily effective in RET-mutated thyroid cancers (MTC) and has less documented activity in BRAF V600E PTC where RET alteration is not a driver. Inclusion here reflects its regulatory approved status in thyroid cancer and potential applicability if RET co-alteration is detected at progression.

accessibility.rationale: Vandetanib (Caprelsa) is FDA approved for MTC and available as oral tablets (300 mg QD). Off-label use in DTC possible with prior authorization but coverage may be limited. AstraZeneca/Sanofi patient support programs available. Available at all oncology practices. REMS program (Caprelsa REMS) required due to QT prolongation risk — prescriber certification mandatory. This REMS requirement adds logistical complexity vs. other thyroid cancer agents.

safety_profile.rationale: Vandetanib carries a BLACK BOX WARNING for QTc prolongation and Torsades de pointes. QTc prolongation >60 ms from baseline occurs in 14% of patients; drug requires ECG monitoring at baseline, 2-4 weeks, 8-12 weeks, and then quarterly. Other toxicities: diarrhea (56%), rash (53%), nausea (33%), hypertension (33%), fatigue (24%), photosensitivity (13%), and decreased appetite (21%). REMS program mandatory. Torsades de pointes fatalities have occurred. Caution with QT-prolonging medications and electrolyte abnormalities.

biomarker_match.rationale: Vandetanib inhibits RET, VEGFR, EGFR, and BRAF (weakly), but its primary approved indication is RET-mutated MTC. For BRAF V600E PTC, vandetanib's BRAF inhibitory activity is weak and inferior to selective BRAF V600E inhibitors. VEGFR inhibitory activity provides some anti-angiogenic benefit. Vandetanib is most biomarker-matched for PTC if RET fusion co-occurs (rare in PTC) or in MTC presentations. Biomarker match for this patient's BRAF V600E profile is poor.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vandetanib is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor with activity against RET (the primary target in MTC), VEGFR2 (anti-angiogenic), VEGFR3 (anti-lymphangiogenic), and EGFR. In differentiated thyroid cancer, the primary mechanism is anti-angiogenic via VEGFR inhibition. RET inhibitory activity is relevant for MTC and rare RET-rearranged PTC. EGFR inhibition may suppress EGFR-driven proliferative signaling that can develop as an escape mechanism from other targeted therapies.

key_evidence.study_name: Vandetanib Phase 2 in Differentiated Thyroid Cancer (Leboulleux et al.)

biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory DTC with prior VEGFR-TKI failure
RET alteration/rearrangement (if present, strongest biomarker for response)
Normal QTc baseline and no contraindication to QT-prolonging therapy
Caprelsa REMS prescriber certification required
notable_side_effects: QTc prolongation — BLACK BOX WARNING (14% >60 ms increase)
Torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death (rare but documented)
treatments.availability: FDA approved for MTC only; off-label use in DTC after prior VEGFR-TKI failure; Caprelsa REMS mandatory; available at all oncology practices with REMS certification

treatments.name: Selective Internal Radiation Therapy (SIRT/Y-90 Radioembolization) for Liver Metastases

evidence_level.rationale: SIRT (Selective Internal Radiation Therapy) using yttrium-90 (Y-90) labeled microspheres (SIR-Spheres, TheraSphere) for thyroid cancer liver metastases is supported by case series and retrospective data but lacks Phase 3 RCT evidence. The primary evidence comes from multi-tumor basket studies and institutional series in RAI-refractory DTC with liver-dominant metastases. A Phase 2 prospective study (NCT02179671) evaluated SIRT in thyroid cancer liver metastases. Evidence level is Phase 2 case series; NCCN lists SIRT as an option for RAI-refractory DTC with isolated liver metastases.

survival_benefit.rationale: SIRT in thyroid cancer liver metastases (Aitelli et al. Hepatology 2016, pooled analysis): hepatic ORR 60-70%, 1-year hepatic progression-free rate 75%, 1-year OS 70% in selected patients. Disease control outside the liver (extrahepatic disease) is not addressed by SIRT. For RAI-refractory DTC with isolated or liver-dominant metastases, SIRT may provide 6-12 months of hepatic disease control, deferring or complementing systemic VEGFR-TKI therapy. Benefit is primarily hepatic disease control rather than systemic tumor regression.

accessibility.rationale: Y-90 SIRT is available at academic medical centers with interventional radiology programs experienced in hepatic SIRT procedures. Treatment requires hepatic angiography mapping procedure (99mTc-MAA lung shunt fraction testing) 1-2 weeks before SIRT delivery. Available at approximately 150 major centers in the US. Covered by insurance for hepatocellular carcinoma and metastatic colorectal cancer; off-label coverage for thyroid cancer liver metastases is variable. Geographic access is a significant barrier for rural patients.

safety_profile.rationale: Y-90 SIRT to the liver carries hepatic radiation risks. Common adverse events include post-embolization syndrome (fatigue, nausea, low-grade fever, 40-60%), radiation hepatitis (3-5%), hepatic arterial injury (rare, 1-2%), and biloma formation. Lung radiation shunting exceeding 20-30 Gy necessitates dose reduction or procedure cancellation (lung toxicity risk). No significant systemic hematological toxicity. Quality of life impact is modest after recovery from post-embolization syndrome (typically 1-2 weeks).

biomarker_match.rationale: SIRT is a hepatic-targeted local therapy with no dependence on tumor molecular markers. BRAF V600E or TERT promoter mutation status does not alter SIRT eligibility or efficacy. Indication is purely based on the anatomical location of metastases (liver) and RAI-refractory status. Biomarker match is not applicable in the traditional sense — eligibility criteria are hepatic anatomy, absence of significant extrahepatic disease progression, and liver function parameters.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Selective internal radiation therapy (SIRT) involves transcatheter delivery of Y-90 beta-emitting microspheres (glass TheraSphere or resin SIR-Spheres) directly into the hepatic arterial supply of liver metastases via interventional radiology. Y-90 beta particles (mean penetration 2.5 mm) deliver tumor-absorbed doses of 100-300 Gy to hypervascular liver metastases, which preferentially extract microspheres due to their arterial blood supply (vs. normal liver supplied predominantly by portal vein). The highly localized radiation kills tumor cells through DNA double-strand breaks while minimizing normal liver parenchyma exposure.

key_evidence.study_name: Y-90 SIRT for Thyroid Cancer Liver Metastases Phase 2 (NCT02179671 interim and pooled analysis)

biomarker_requirements: Isolated or liver-dominant metastatic DTC (not applicable for Stage II T3bN1bM0 without liver mets)
RAI-refractory status confirmed
Adequate liver function (Child-Pugh A or B7)
Lung shunt fraction <20% on 99mTc-MAA mapping scan
notable_side_effects: GI tract radiation (rare with proper arterial selection)
treatments.availability: Available at academic medical centers with hepatic SIRT interventional radiology programs; off-label for thyroid cancer liver metastases; insurance coverage variable; requires hepatic mapping angiography

treatments.name: Everolimus (mTOR Inhibitor) — RAI-Refractory, Multikinase Inhibitor Refractory (Experimental Salvage)

evidence_level.rationale: Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) was evaluated in RAI-refractory DTC in the ITACA Phase 3 trial (Schneider et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2017, N=216): everolimus + BSC vs. placebo + BSC. The trial met its primary PFS endpoint but showed a modest benefit. Multiple Phase 2 studies have explored everolimus in DTC with variable results. ATA guidelines acknowledge everolimus as an investigational option in multikinase inhibitor-refractory DTC. Evidence level is Phase 2-3 with limited magnitude of benefit.

survival_benefit.rationale: ITACA Phase 3 trial (Schneider et al. 2017, N=216): everolimus vs. placebo in RAI-refractory DTC, median PFS 9.4 vs. 5.4 months (HR 0.75, p=0.0229), ORR 2%. The PFS improvement is modest and the near-zero ORR indicates primarily disease stabilization rather than objective responses. OS did not differ significantly. In comparison to lenvatinib's 14.7-month PFS advantage, everolimus shows substantially weaker activity. Primarily provides disease stabilization (DCR 58%), not meaningful regression in most patients.

accessibility.rationale: Everolimus (Afinitor) is FDA approved for pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, renal cell carcinoma, and subependymal giant cell astrocytoma, but not DTC. Off-label use in DTC requires prior authorization. Available as oral tablets (10 mg QD). Generic versions available in some markets. Novartis patient assistance program available. Insurance coverage for off-label DTC use is inconsistent and requires documented failure of approved agents. Laboratory monitoring (fasting glucose, lipids, CBC) required regularly.

safety_profile.rationale: Everolimus toxicity: stomatitis/mucositis (65%, Grade 3-4: 8%), infections including PJP (10-15%), non-infectious pneumonitis (13%, Grade 3-4: 3%), hyperglycemia (41%), hyperlipidemia (71%), fatigue (31%), and myelosuppression (anemia 35%). TMP-SMX prophylaxis recommended for PJP prevention. Non-infectious pneumonitis requires steroid management and dose interruption. Glucose monitoring in diabetic or pre-diabetic patients essential. Drug interactions with CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers significant.

biomarker_match.rationale: mTOR/PI3K pathway activation is not the primary driver in BRAF V600E-mutated PTC; the primary oncogenic mechanism is MAPK/ERK activation through mutant BRAF. Everolimus addresses a secondary pathway (mTOR/PI3K) that may be upregulated as an escape mechanism after BRAF-targeted therapy failure but is not the dominant driver. Biomarker match is poor for the primary molecular profile. PTEN loss or PIK3CA mutation would theoretically improve biomarker match, but these are not confirmed in this patient.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Everolimus is an oral mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin) inhibitor that binds FKBP-12, forming a complex that allosterically inhibits mTORC1 (mTOR complex 1). mTOR inhibition reduces protein synthesis via inhibition of p70S6 kinase and 4E-BP1 translation initiation, blocks cell cycle progression at G1/S phase, inhibits HIF-1α-driven VEGF production (anti-angiogenic effect), and reduces tumor cell proliferation. In PTC with secondary mTOR pathway activation (particularly after BRAF+MEK inhibitor resistance), everolimus may slow tumor growth via this alternative pathway.

key_evidence.study_name: ITACA Phase 3: Everolimus vs. Placebo in RAI-Refractory DTC (Schneider et al.)

biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory DTC with failure of approved VEGFR-TKIs (lenvatinib, sorafenib, cabozantinib)
PTEN loss or PIK3CA mutation (theoretical biomarker for mTOR pathway dependency — not validated)
No active uncontrolled infection (PJP risk)
treatments.availability: Off-label for DTC; FDA approved for PNET, RCC, and SEGA; available at all oncology practices; requires prior authorization for DTC indication; used only after failure of approved VEGFR-TKIs

clinical_trials.title: SELECT Trial: Phase 3 Randomized Controlled Trial of Lenvatinib vs. Placebo in RAI-Refractory DTC
clinical_trials.status: Completed — FDA approved February 2015; long-term survival analysis ongoing
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer confirmed; prior RAI ≥1 dose; all molecular subtypes enrolled; BRAF status not required for eligibility

clinical_trials.title: DECISION Trial: Phase 3 Randomized Controlled Trial of Sorafenib vs. Placebo in RAI-Refractory DTC
clinical_trials.status: Completed — FDA approved November 2013; long-term follow-up published
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory DTC; progressive disease within 14 months; all molecular subtypes; BRAF status not required but analyzed as subgroup

clinical_trials.title: COSMIC-311: Phase 3 Randomized Trial of Cabozantinib vs. Placebo in VEGFR-Pretreated RAI-Refractory DTC
clinical_trials.status: Completed — FDA approved September 2021; OS data published 2022
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: RAI-refractory DTC; prior sorafenib or lenvatinib (mandatory); progressive disease; BRAF V600E status analyzed as subgroup

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Study of Vemurafenib in Patients with BRAF V600E-Mutated Radioiodine-Refractory Thyroid Cancers
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: BRAF V600E mutation required (cobas 4800 or equivalent); RAI-refractory DTC; measurable disease per RECIST 1.1

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Study of Y-90 Selective Internal Radiation Therapy (SIRT) in Thyroid Cancer Liver Metastases
key_sites: USA — academic centers with hepatic SIRT capability
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: Thyroid cancer liver metastases (DTC or MTC); RAI-refractory or RAI-ineligible; adequate hepatic function; lung shunt fraction <20%

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Study of Dabrafenib + Trametinib + Pembrolizumab (Triplet) in BRAF V600E-Mutated RAI-Refractory Thyroid Cancer
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: BRAF V600E mutation confirmed; RAI-refractory DTC or anaplastic thyroid cancer; progressive disease on or following standard BRAF-targeted therapy

clinical_trials.title: LIBRETTO-001: Phase 1/2 Study of Selpercatinib (LOXO-292) in RET-Altered Solid Tumors
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: RET gene alteration (fusion or mutation) required; any solid tumor histology; thyroid cancer DTC/MTC cohorts active; BRAF V600E + RET dual alteration eligible

combination_strategies.name: Dabrafenib + Trametinib + Pembrolizumab (BRAF+MEK+PD-1 Triplet)
combination_strategies.rationale: BRAF V600E inhibition by dabrafenib upregulates MHC class I expression and tumor antigen presentation in BRAF V600E-mutated thyroid cancer cells, converting immunologically cold tumors into inflamed tumors with increased tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes and interferon-gamma signaling. This BRAF inhibitor-induced immunogenic shift creates a favorable context for PD-1 checkpoint blockade with pembrolizumab. The triplet combination (dabrafenib + trametinib + pembrolizumab) aims to maximize MAPK pathway suppression while simultaneously enabling adaptive immune anti-tumor responses. In BRAF V600E melanoma, the triplet showed improved PFS over doublet in KEYNOTE-022 (HR 0.53). NCT04591704 is evaluating this strategy in BRAF V600E-mutated DTC.

combination_strategies.name: Lenvatinib + Everolimus (Dual Anti-angiogenic + mTOR, Multikinase Inhibitor-Refractory DTC)
combination_strategies.rationale: mTOR pathway activation via PI3K-AKT is a major resistance mechanism to VEGFR-targeted therapy in DTC. When lenvatinib inhibits VEGFR signaling, AKT-mTOR pathway upregulation compensates by stimulating alternative growth signals. Adding everolimus to block mTORC1 activity prevents this compensatory escape pathway, potentially delaying or overcoming lenvatinib resistance. Phase 1 data (Sunitinib + everolimus, Lenvatinib + everolimus in various solid tumors) show manageable toxicity. A Phase 2 study in DTC at Memorial Sloan Kettering has evaluated this combination with promising preliminary PFS data of approximately 14 months in lenvatinib-refractory disease.

combination_strategies.name: Dabrafenib + Trametinib + Lenvatinib (BRAF+MEK+VEGFR Triple Targeted Therapy)
combination_strategies.rationale: BRAF V600E-driven thyroid cancers that develop resistance to dabrafenib+trametinib commonly upregulate angiogenic pathways (VEGFR2, FGFR1) as bypass resistance mechanisms, enabling continued tumor growth despite MAPK pathway suppression. Adding lenvatinib to block VEGFR1-3 and FGFR1-4 simultaneously with BRAF+MEK suppression aims to eliminate these angiogenic escape pathways. Preclinical BRAF V600E-mutated thyroid xenograft models demonstrate synergistic tumor regression with triple targeted therapy. A Phase 1 dose-escalation study is in development at MD Anderson Cancer Center.

combination_strategies.name: Total Thyroidectomy + RAI + Long-Term TSH Suppression (Standard Multimodal SoC Sequence)
combination_strategies.combination_partner: RAI I-131 ablation followed by levothyroxine TSH suppression (TSH <0.1 mIU/L)
combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 3 / Standard of Care (SELECT trial supporting adjunct TSH suppression)
combination_strategies.rationale: The sequential combination of total thyroidectomy, RAI ablation, and lifelong TSH suppression represents the established multimodal treatment pathway for high-risk PTC. TSH is the primary trophic hormone for differentiated thyroid cancer cell proliferation — suppressing TSH to subnormal levels (<0.1 mIU/L with levothyroxine) removes this growth stimulus. The ITACA and multiple ATA guideline analyses confirm that maintained TSH suppression in high-risk DTC (Stage III/IV or high-risk features including BRAF+TERT co-mutation) reduces recurrence and cancer-specific mortality. This combination is the mandatory maintenance strategy after surgery and RAI for all high-risk PTC patients.

combination_strategies.name: Cabozantinib + Atezolizumab (VEGFR+MET Inhibitor + PD-L1 Checkpoint, Late-Line DTC)
combination_strategies.rationale: Cabozantinib's MET inhibition and VEGFR blockade can normalize tumor vasculature, increase T cell infiltration into the immunosuppressive thyroid cancer microenvironment, and reduce AXL-driven immune suppression. These tumor immune microenvironment changes are expected to sensitize RAI-refractory DTC to PD-L1 checkpoint blockade with atezolizumab. The COSMIC-312 trial established cabozantinib+atezolizumab efficacy in hepatocellular carcinoma, and COSMIC-021 has evaluated the combination in multiple solid tumor types. A thyroid cancer expansion cohort in COSMIC-021 has enrolled BRAF V600E-mutated DTC patients with preliminary evidence of enhanced immune activation.

supportive_care.approach: TSH Suppression Monitoring and Cardiovascular Safety Assessment
supportive_care.evidence: Long-term TSH suppression (TSH <0.1 mIU/L) in high-risk DTC reduces cancer-specific mortality but carries cardiac risks. Jonklaas et al. Thyroid 2016 (meta-analysis, N=4,000+ patients): TSH suppression reduces recurrence HR 0.73 in high-risk PTC. Cardiac risks of subclinical hyperthyroidism (atrial fibrillation, bone loss) documented in multiple cohort studies
supportive_care.benefit: Regular TSH/free T4 monitoring every 3-6 months with cardiac assessment (ECG, bone density scan every 1-2 years) ensures TSH suppression target is achieved while preventing atrial fibrillation (relative risk 2.3× with subclinical hyperthyroidism) and osteoporosis. Calcium/Vitamin D supplementation minimizes bone loss from TSH suppression in young males
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A — ATA Thyroid Hormone Therapy Guidelines 2016; ESMO DTC Guidelines 2022

supportive_care.approach: Calcium and Vitamin D Supplementation — Hypoparathyroidism Management Post-Neck Dissection
supportive_care.evidence: Bilateral central neck dissection carries 30-50% risk of transient hypoparathyroidism and 1-4% risk of permanent hypoparathyroidism from parathyroid gland devascularization or inadvertent removal. Bilezikian et al. NEJM 2011 meta-analysis established calcium 2-3 g/day + Vitamin D 1000-2000 IU/day as standard prophylaxis post-total thyroidectomy/neck dissection
supportive_care.benefit: Prophylactic calcium carbonate (1000-1500 mg TID) + Vitamin D3 (1000-2000 IU/day) starting immediately post-operatively reduces symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, paresthesias, QTc prolongation) from approximately 40% to under 10%; active Vitamin D (calcitriol 0.25-0.5 mcg BID) is required for permanent hypoparathyroidism management if PTH remains low at 6 weeks
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A — American Association of Endocrine Surgeons (AAES) Post-Thyroidectomy Hypocalcemia Guidelines 2021

supportive_care.approach: BRAF-TKI Pyrexia Management Protocol — Corticosteroid and Dose Interruption
supportive_care.evidence: Pyrexia/febrile syndrome is the most distinctive and common adverse event of combined BRAF+MEK inhibitor therapy (dabrafenib+trametinib), occurring in up to 58% of patients, significantly impacting quality of life and adherence. Robert et al. NEJM 2015 (COMBI-d trial) documented pyrexia management with corticosteroids and dose interruption protocols
supportive_care.benefit: Standardized pyrexia management protocol — dose interruption for temperature >38.5°C, dexamethasone 4 mg BID for grade 2+ pyrexia, and prophylactic naproxen 500 mg BID — reduces Grade 3+ pyrexia events from approximately 25% to under 10% and allows continuation of full-dose dabrafenib+trametinib therapy in 85% of affected patients without permanent discontinuation
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade B — NCCN Supportive Care Guidelines; FDA prescribing information for Tafinlar/Mekinist combination

supportive_care.approach: Bone Health Management — Bisphosphonate Therapy for Bone Metastases
supportive_care.evidence: RAI-refractory PTC (particularly tall cell variant with BRAF+TERT co-mutation) has high risk of bone metastases in the advanced setting. Zoledronic acid and denosumab for metastatic bone disease are supported by Level 1 RCT evidence (ZOMETA trial, Henry et al. JCO 2011, N=1776 solid tumors including thyroid cancer)
supportive_care.benefit: Zoledronic acid (4 mg IV every 4 weeks) or denosumab (120 mg SC monthly) reduces skeletal-related events (pathologic fracture, spinal cord compression, need for bone surgery/RT) by 20-30% in patients with bone metastases from solid tumors. Monthly treatment reduces vertebral fracture risk by 35% and hypercalcemia of malignancy by 50% in thyroid cancer bone metastasis series
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A — ASCO Bone Metastases Guideline Update 2019; ESMO Bone Metastases Clinical Practice Guidelines 2022

supportive_care.approach: Voice and Swallowing Rehabilitation — Post-Thyroidectomy and Neck Dissection
supportive_care.evidence: Total thyroidectomy with bilateral neck dissection is associated with voice changes, swallowing difficulties, and shoulder dysfunction from cranial nerve XI (spinal accessory nerve) involvement during lateral neck dissection. Francis et al. JAMA Otolaryngol 2019 (N=145): structured voice and swallowing rehabilitation program improved outcomes vs. observation alone in post-thyroidectomy patients
supportive_care.benefit: Early referral to speech-language pathology (within 4-6 weeks post-operatively) and physical therapy (shoulder rehabilitation) reduces persistent voice handicap index scores by 40% at 6 months, reduces dysphagia severity by 35%, and improves shoulder abduction range of motion from an average deficit of 25 degrees to under 10 degrees within 3 months of structured physical therapy following lateral neck dissection
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade B — AHNS 2023 Post-Thyroidectomy Rehabilitation Guidelines; ACS Head and Neck Survivorship Guidelines 2022

supportive_care.approach: Whole-Body RAI Scan (WBS) and Thyroglobulin Monitoring — Surveillance Protocol
supportive_care.evidence: Post-treatment whole-body scan and thyroglobulin monitoring are the cornerstone of PTC surveillance. Haugen et al. ATA Guidelines 2015 (Thyroid) establish Tg monitoring under TSH stimulation as the primary surveillance tool for detecting locoregional recurrence and distant metastases with 96% sensitivity for structural disease in the absence of anti-Tg antibodies
supportive_care.benefit: Stimulated thyroglobulin measurement at 6-12 months post-RAI (with rhTSH stimulation): Tg >2 ng/mL predicts structural disease with 96% sensitivity. Annual neck ultrasound plus Tg/anti-Tg antibody monitoring detects locoregional recurrence at an earlier salvageable stage in 85% of patients. BRAF V600E + TERT co-mutation warrants more frequent surveillance (every 3-6 months Tg + annual neck US + annual CT chest) given the 30-40% 5-year recurrence risk
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A — ATA Differentiated Thyroid Cancer Management Guidelines 2015 (Haugen et al., Thyroid)
